title: Acriflavine Direct Detection Technique for Labyrinthulomycetes
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"5lcev","text":"This protocol is modified from: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3p7v5","text":"Raghukumar S, Schaumann K. 1993. An epifluorescence microscopy method for direct\ndetection and enumeration of the fungilike marine protists, the thraustochytrids. Limnol.\nOceanogr. 38(1): 182-187.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Collect 100 μl of live cells on a 0.22 μm membrane filter. This protocol works well for Schizochytrium 28209 growing in 1/10 790 or 1/2 790; the volume filtered can be adjusted to accomodate the culture cell density. The cells can also be fixed with formaldehyde; we have not observed a big difference in fresh vs fixed cells.
Rinse cells on filter with filter-sterilized artifical seawater. We generally use half-strength artificial seawater.

Add 3-4 mL of 0.05% acriflavine in 0.1 M citrate buffer at pH 3.0 to the cells and let stand for 4 minutes. A 0.5% stock solution of acriflavine in distilled water is used to make the 0.05% acrflavine working solution.
Vacuum drain the stain and once it is fully drained, add 2 ml of 75% isopropyl alcohol while still drawing the vacuum.
Once all isopropyl alcohol is drained, rinse filter with sterile distilled water.
Place filter on microscope slide, add a drop of water (or immersion oil) on it and place cover slip on top.

Under epiflourescence microscopy, labyrinthulomycete cells should have a red-fluorescent cell wall and green-fluorescent cytoplasm. In our cultures, the cell wall is often thin and hard to see. Preferred excitation filters: 420-490 nm violet-to-blue or 450-490 nm blue.
authors: This protocol is modified from:  Raghukumar S
Schaumann K. 1993. An epifluorescence microscopy method for direct detection and enumeration of the
